topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, the paper slightly yellowed at the edges, and the handwriting on the front was elegant, almost painfully so. Elias stared at it under the harsh glare of the kitchen light, his hand trembling so badly he had to grip the counter for support. It was from Clara. Seven years. Seven years since the night everything fractured, since the silence between them had become a chasm too wide to cross. He didn't need the words; he already knew what they would say—the clumsy excuses, the fleeting regrets. He tore the seal open with a ragged breath, the sound loud in the quiet apartment. Inside, the letter was short, a carefully constructed apology for a moment he couldn't rewind. Elias read it twice, his eyes scanning the familiar script, searching for the lie, the hidden sting. When he finally lowered the paper, a low, guttural sound escaped his throat, a sound more animal than human. He crumpled the letter slowly, deliberately, then smoothed it out again, his knuckles white against the cheap paper, a frantic energy thrumming beneath his skin.

[story 2]

Maya traced the embossed lettering on the stationary with a fingertip, the texture cool and unforgiving against her skin. It had arrived on a Tuesday, completely unannounced, tucked beneath the stack of overdue bills. Years had passed since the argument, years filled with the dull ache of absence. She reread the prose, the careful phrasing about understanding and remorse. Her chest felt tight, a physical constriction that made drawing a full breath an effort. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room floor, her movements jerky and uneven. Every instinct screamed at her to throw the letter across the room, to destroy the evidence of this unwelcome contact. But she couldn't. Her gaze kept returning to the ink, a strange, hot pressure building behind her eyes. She sank onto the sofa, burying her face in her hands, letting out a shuddering exhale that seemed to empty her lungs entirely.

[story 3]

The postman had dropped the package off without a word, just a slip of cream-colored paper addressed to him. When Liam finally retrieved it, the weight of it felt immense, crushing him against the doorframe. It was from Sarah. It was an apology, dated nearly a decade ago, for a careless word spoken in the heat of a terrible misunderstanding. Liam didn't open it immediately. He just stood there, his jaw clenched so tightly he could feel the muscles protesting in his neck. He paced the length of the hallway, his footsteps echoing hollowly. When he finally unfolded the paper, the words swam before his eyes. A strange, hot liquid welled up in his throat, and he had to swallow hard to keep from choking on the sudden surge of emotion. He let the letter fall to the floor, not caring about the sound, and knelt down, picking it up again, his hands shaking uncontrollably as he absorbed the belated acknowledgment.
